metadata.title: Causal Inference — Confounding Audit for ML Models
metadata.topic: CAUSAL INFERENCE — CONFOUNDING AUDIT FOR ML MODELS

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: It stuns me that the crimson SPURIOUS bar at point four five sits above the green CAUSAL signal at point three eight, purely because data-collection bias inflated its correlation to the label. I want to prove the coloring earns its keep. Help me: (1) retrain the logistic regression with the spurious feature dropped and measure how test accuracy on out-of-distribution data changes; (2) vary the confounder's correlation from point three to point nine and watch its SHAP rank climb; (3) have Claude re-justify each classification given the shifted numbers.

props.command: It stuns me that the crimson SPURIOUS bar at point four five sits above the green CAUSAL signal at point three eight, purely because data-collection bias inflated its correlation to the label. I want to prove the coloring earns its keep. Help me: (1) retrain the logistic regression with the spurious feature dropped and measure how test accuracy on out-of-distribution data changes; (2) vary the confounder's correlation from point three to point nine and watch its SHAP rank climb; (3) have Claude re-justify each classification given the shifted numbers.
props.topic: CAUSAL INFERENCE — CONFOUNDING AUDIT FOR ML MODELS

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Your SHAP bar chart shows the top feature with an importance score of point four five. You optimize for it. But what if that feature is a spurious confounder — correlated with the label through data-collection bias, not through any causal mechanism? High SHAP importance does not mean causal relevance. The confounding audit classifies each feature as causally plausible, spurious, or unknown before you trust the importances.
beats.visual_intent: title card: high SHAP score vs causal plausibility — 'importance is not causation'; the risk of optimizing on spurious features

beats.narration_text: Into the terminal: claude — build a Python confounding audit for an ML classifier: generate N equals five thousand examples with a spurious confounder correlated at point seven with the label via data-collection bias and a true signal; train logistic regression; compute SHAP values; call Claude to classify each feature as causally plausible, spurious, or unknown; output a color-coded SHAP bar chart.

props.topic: CAUSAL INFERENCE — CONFOUNDING AUDIT FOR ML MODELS

beats.narration_text: Read it. The audit call passes the SHAP values and feature names to Claude with a domain description: what is the data, what is the label, what is the collection process? Claude returns a causal classification for each feature. The classification drives the color coding — not the SHAP score.

props.topic: CAUSAL INFERENCE — CONFOUNDING AUDIT FOR ML MODELS
props.code:
# confound_audit.py — Claude Code output
import shap, numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# SHAP values
explainer   = shap.LinearExplainer(clf, X_train)
shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_test)
mean_shap   = np.abs(shap_values).mean(axis=0)

# Causal audit via Claude
claude_audit = classify_features_causally(
    shap_values, feature_names, domain_description)
# -> {'spurious_confounder': 'SPURIOUS',
#     'true_signal':         'CAUSAL',
#     'noise_feat_1':        'unknown',
#     'noise_feat_2':        'unknown'}

beats.narration_text: Run it. The SHAP bar chart builds out. The spurious confounder has the highest score — point four five — but it is colored crimson and labeled SPURIOUS. The true signal is second at point three eight, colored green, labeled CAUSAL. Two noise features round it out at point one two and point zero eight. The highest bar is the one you should trust least.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: vertical SHAP bar chart — spurious_confounder tallest (CRIMSON, SPURIOUS), true_signal second (PASS_CLR, CAUSAL), two noise features (SLATE); annotation 'High SHAP != Causal feature'

beats.narration_text: The spurious confounder is dominant because data collection happened during a biased period — specific demographics were over-sampled. Iterate: re-generate the data with stratified sampling to correct the collection bias, retrain, and re-run the SHAP audit.

props.command:
claude "update confound_audit.py:
  -> re-generate data with stratified sampling
     to correct collection bias
  -> retrain LogisticRegression
  -> re-run SHAP + causal audit
  -> does spurious_confounder SHAP drop?"
props.topic: CAUSAL INFERENCE — CONFOUNDING AUDIT FOR ML MODELS

beats.narration_text: Re-run. After stratified resampling, the spurious confounder's SHAP score drops to point one five — now below the true signal at point three eight. The bias is gone from the data, and the model's importances realign with causation.
beats.visual_intent: screen-recording or slate: terminal showing re-run SHAP values — spurious_confounder drops, true_signal becomes dominant; confound audit confirms SPURIOUS still but with low importance

beats.narration_text: High SHAP importance is not a causal certificate. A spurious confounder can dominate the importance chart through data-collection bias alone, with no causal link to the outcome. The audit classifies before you act — so you do not optimize a model on a phantom.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: spurious_confounder vs true_signal; 'classification before optimization' as the principle; SHAP score vs causal plausibility

beats.narration_text: Your move: run the causal audit on your top ten SHAP features. For each one, ask: could this feature be correlated with the label through a data-collection mechanism rather than a causal one? If yes, test what happens when you drop it from training — if performance holds, it was a confounder.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) audit your top 10 SHAP features causally, 2) ask data-collection mechanism question for each, 3) drop and retrain to test

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. High SHAP importance is not a causal certificate. A spurious confounder can dominate the importance chart through data-collection bias alone, with no causal link to the outcome. The audit classifies before you act — so you do not optimize a model on a phantom. Your move: run the causal audit on your top ten SHAP features. For each one, ask: could this feature be correlated with the label through a data-collection mechanism rather than a causal one? If yes, test what happens when you drop it from training — if performance holds, it was a confounder.

beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
